content.greeting: Hi there! I wanted to share some exciting updates about how Lily is doing with her social communication goal this semester.
highlights: Lily's ability to stay on a peer's conversation topic has improved significantly  -  she went from 2 exchanges to 4+ exchanges in one-on-one conversations. She had a wonderful conversation with her friend Mia about their pets last month that really showed growth.

She noticed a classmate was upset at recess last month and asked 'are you okay?' completely on her own. This was a huge moment  -  reading body language and responding with empathy is exactly what we've been working toward.
During a group project this week, Lily asked a peer 'what do you think?' for the first time. She's starting to invite others into conversations instead of only sharing her own ideas.
Her emotion recognition from facial and body cues has gone from about 40% to 75% accuracy. She's really internalizing the 'body clue' checklist we use in social skills group.
try_at_home: When Lily talks about octopuses at dinner (and she will!), try gently asking: 'That's so cool  -  now tell me about YOUR day first, and then I want to hear the octopus fact.' This mirrors our 'topic tracker' approach at school.

Practice the 'body clue' game  -  show Lily photos or pause a TV show and ask 'how is this person feeling? What clues do you see?' She's great with happy and sad; work on frustrated, nervous, and embarrassed.

If Lily has a playdate, suggest a structured activity first (board game, Minecraft together) rather than open-ended free play. She does much better in conversations when there's a shared activity to anchor the talk.
content.closing: Lily is making real, steady progress. Group conversations with more than 2 people are still our biggest challenge, but her one-on-one skills have grown so much. She's a thoughtful kid who genuinely wants to connect  -  she's just building her toolkit. Please let me know if you have any questions or see anything different at home!
